jokes.text: Ilana interrupts the nude modeling session to chat with Abbi, and Abbi reacts with delighted shock: 'Oh, my God, I'm a model?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is told 'models have to be silent,' implying she has been chatting loudly in the middle of an art class where her naked friend is posing.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's gleeful, child-like realization that she is in fact a model — her vanity and naivety condensed into three words.
jokes.explanation: Tight economy and strong character specificity — Abbi's earnest self-regard is the core of her character. The joke is small but clean. The instructor's reprimand setting up Abbi's delight is well-structured.

jokes.text: Instructor calls Abbi 'Ali' and Abbi corrects her — 'It's Abbi, remember, we talked about that' — even mid-cover-up.
jokes.setup: Instructor intervenes in the disruption.
jokes.punchline: Abbi, naked and in a compromised social position, prioritizes correcting her name — a recurring anxiety-driven compulsion.
jokes.explanation: Classic Abbi specificity — her need to be acknowledged correctly overrides situational embarrassment. Economy is strong ('remember, we talked about that' implies prior attempts). Feels earned.

jokes.text: When the instructor asks if there's a problem, Ilana says they're 'strategizing like the shading... of the boobs.'
jokes.setup: Instructor challenges Ilana for disrupting the class.
jokes.punchline: Ilana fabricates an artistic justification that involves the word 'boobs' with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy — Ilana's brazen confidence in absurd improvisations is her defining trait. The trailing off ('of the boobs') is the best part, the word landing as both a euphemism and a literal description.

jokes.text: Abbi's co-worker says wisdom tooth removal photos gave her 'huge chipmunk cheeks' at prom, then Abbi says 'That's so cute' — then immediately suggests the surgery will 'make more room in my mouth, right?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is nervously sharing she's having wisdom teeth surgery. The co-worker shares a relatable prom anecdote.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's innocent comment about 'more room in my mouth' is the setup for a sexual misread she immediately has to walk back.
jokes.explanation: Classic double-entendre structure. The execution is fine — the 'right?' trailing question is suitably oblivious. But this is a familiar bit and the character-specificity is low; anyone could make this gaffe.

jokes.text: Abbi immediately backtracks: 'No, I didn't mean, um, blow jobs.' Then doubles down: 'Although, if that is true, that it would make more room in my mouth. I didn't do them. I enjoy it both ways.'
jokes.setup: Abbi realizes her 'more room in my mouth' comment has been heard sexually.
jokes.punchline: Her attempt to backtrack accelerates into a multi-sentence spiral of oversharing that somehow ends with her claiming she 'enjoys it both ways' — on a completely unrelated register.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is the engine here — each sentence makes things worse, which is good cringe comedy. 'I enjoy it both ways' is the best line, shifting from denial to accidental advocacy. The spiral is characteristically Abbi.

jokes.text: In fact, I'm getting a call, it's ringing. Busy day. Hello? One sec, Jeremy... just give me one... Yup? Well, my laundry is in the bag!
jokes.setup: Abbi is mid-awkward conversation about blow jobs and tries to escape by pretending to take a phone call
jokes.punchline: Her fake call escalates into a full dramatic laundry dispute — 'Why would someone say that? Why would they say... It's my laundry man and then he's upset'
jokes.explanation: The bit is Abbi improvising an escape from an awkward conversation by inventing an elaborate fake call — classic social-anxiety comedy. The specificity of 'my laundry man' and 'stains on my underwear' is funny because it's oddly plausible as a real Abbi concern.

jokes.text: Abbi, trying to escape the conversation, pretends to answer a call from 'Jeremy' about a laundry dispute, then her fake angry phone performance escalates into: 'So you better lawyer up, 'cause I'm gonna... kill myself right now.'
jokes.setup: Abbi invents a phone call to flee the awkward blow job conversation.
jokes.punchline: The fake phone argument escalates absurdly until she accidentally says she'll 'kill myself right now' — a comedically dark non-sequitur that deflates the whole fabrication.
jokes.explanation: The dark landing is genuinely surprising. The long build-up of the fake angry phone call makes the suicidal non-sequitur land harder. The pause before it is the key beat. Abbi painting herself into an increasingly absurd corner is very much her character.

jokes.text: Ilana arrives at the dentist, cradles Abbi like a baby, calls herself 'Mommy Lani,' and hands Abbi a stuffed animal called 'Bingo Bronson that Mommy Lani got you.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is pre-op, nervous, and vulnerable.
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately reframes the dynamic as maternal, with a baby voice, a stuffed animal, and a full name ('Bingo Bronson') that suggests she put real thought into it.
jokes.explanation: 'Bingo Bronson' alone is a brilliantly specific comedy detail — it suggests Ilana spent genuine time naming a stuffed animal for her friend's dental surgery. The 'Me, I'm Mommy Lani' clarification doubles down on the absurdity perfectly. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana asks the dentist: 'Mayonnaise clinic claims that facial paralysis can be a thing? Care to comment?' while gripping his head.
jokes.setup: Ilana is anxious on Abbi's behalf and interrogating the dentist.
jokes.punchline: 'Mayonnaise clinic' instead of 'Mayo Clinic' — delivered with journalistic aggression while physically restraining a doctor — is pure Ilana malapropism energy.
jokes.explanation: 'Mayonnaise clinic' is inspired — it's a malapropism that perfectly captures Ilana's confident ignorance. 'Release my head, woman' from the doctor is the perfect straight-man cap. The physical comedy of her gripping his head while interrogating him adds a layer. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: The Black dentist tells himself: 'If I mess up this white girl's teeth, the black dentistry game is over... forever. I'm gonna get these teeth. For my people.'
jokes.setup: The dentist is about to perform routine wisdom tooth extraction.
jokes.punchline: He frames it as a high-stakes racial representation crisis — 'the black dentistry game is over forever' — a ludicrously grandiose internal monologue for a standard dental procedure.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it's self-aware about racial stakes in a mundane context. 'For my people' as the punctuation of a dentist's internal prep speech is absurdly funny. Abbi's deadpan 'Wow. I don't do anything for my people' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Abbi, about to go under anesthesia, says 'Wow. I don't do anything for my people.'
jokes.setup: The dentist has just delivered his 'for my people' speech.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's genuine, self-reflective shame about her own lack of communal purpose, expressed in the most inappropriate moment.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Six words that reveal Abbi's self-deprecating, anxious character while also being a genuinely funny non-sequitur response to 'for my people.' The timing — about to go under — makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana narrates Abbi going under like a tearful goodbye: 'Bye, Abbi, I'll see you when you wake up. And if you don't wake up, I'll still see you... 'cause I'm gonna kill myself and meet you in heaven or whatever.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is counting backward into anesthesia.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's loving send-off ends with a casual threat of suicide for companionship in the afterlife — 'or whatever' perfectly deflating the melodrama.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the cold act. The structure is impeccable: the first half is a sincere, touching goodbye, then 'and if you don't wake up' pivots to dark comedy, and 'or whatever' is the perfectly casual landing. 'I'm gonna kill myself and meet you in heaven or whatever' encapsulates Ilana's character — her love for Abbi is genuine but expressed in ways that are functionally insane. It also callbacks Abbi's own accidental 'kill myself' line, making it feel thematically earned rather than random. Rare episode-best-quality.

jokes.text: Ilana, told to calm down in the waiting room, announces: 'I am Abbi's keeper today. I am her mother, sister, father, brother.'
jokes.setup: A medical professional or Lincoln asks Ilana to relax.
jokes.punchline: Ilana defines her role with escalating familial comprehensiveness — she is ALL of Abbi's family, all genders, no exceptions.
jokes.explanation: Functional Ilana character comedy. The four-family-member claim is funny but slightly underdeveloped — it would land harder with a fifth absurd addition. Works as a character beat more than a crafted joke.

jokes.text: Lincoln, watching TV in the waiting room, objects that the one Black character was killed off. Ilana responds 'I don't think these drugs are working, you guys.'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln have been in the waiting room apparently watching a show where Lincoln has noticed the racial representation.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response — 'I don't think these drugs are working' — is either (a) Ilana has taken something in the waiting room, or (b) a complete non-sequitur that ignores Lincoln's valid critique. Either reading is funny.
jokes.explanation: The joke has layers: Lincoln's racial media criticism is real and valid, Ilana's non-response is both dismissive and a revelation that she may have self-medicated. The ambiguity is the best thing about it.

jokes.text: Post-surgery, Ilana tells groggy Abbi: 'It was a huge success, but I had to remove all of your teeth. But don't worry, there's a huge internet community of dudes that like women that are super gum-heavy. It's a huge fetish.'
jokes.setup: Abbi wakes up disoriented from anesthesia.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'bad news / silver lining' delivery: all your teeth are gone, but there's a gum fetish community that will find you attractive. The specificity of 'gum-heavy' is the comedic peak.
jokes.explanation: The word 'gum-heavy' is inspired — it's the kind of specific fake-clinical language that defines great absurdist comedy writing. The structure is perfect: false bad news leading to an even more disturbing consolation. Deeply Ilana — she would absolutely know about this community and think it was reassuring. Genuinely excellent joke that meets the 8.0 anchor bar.

jokes.text: Lincoln breaks the fourth wall on Ilana's gum-fetish lie: 'No, you were here the entire time, you saw... everything.' Followed by Ilana doing a baby voice: 'I am just gwad my wittle baby's awive.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has told Abbi all her teeth were removed.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's exasperated correction, then Ilana pivoting instantly to baby talk instead of apologizing.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's straight-man frustration is a reliable engine. The baby voice pivot avoids accountability in a very Ilana way. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Ilana riffs conspiratorially on the Vicodin prescription: 'Western medicine does stick to a pretty strict schedule. The sooner you take more pills, the sooner you buy more pills, right? Chomp, chomp, chomp, swallow, swallow, swallow. Government-regulated, Doctor Drew all-you-can-eat buffet!'
jokes.setup: The dentist gives strict Vicodin instructions to Ilana.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's anti-pharma conspiracy tangent ending in 'chomp chomp chomp swallow swallow swallow' — a physically vivid and absurd description of pill consumption.
jokes.explanation: The 'chomp chomp swallow' onomatopoeia is the best bit. The anti-pharma rant is slightly bloated but feels very Ilana. 'Doctor Drew all-you-can-eat buffet' is a specific, odd choice of celebrity that adds flavor.

jokes.text: Lincoln, after his pharma rant: 'Uh, but, really, I can handle it.' — delivered in a completely changed, sheepish register.
jokes.setup: Lincoln has just monologued about the corrupt pharmaceutical buffet.
jokes.punchline: He concludes with 'but, really, I can handle it' — a total tonal collapse from conspiratorial grandeur to mild reassurance.
jokes.explanation: A clean button on the previous joke. The tonal whiplash from furious conspiracy to mild competence claim is the whole joke. Functions well but isn't a standalone joke so much as a coda.

jokes.text: Abbi, post-surgery with gauze in her mouth, attempts a Drew Barrymore impression for Ilana.
jokes.setup: Ilana asks if Abbi wants to do her Drew Barrymore impression.
jokes.punchline: The gauze in Abbi's mouth is both the visual gag and the setup — she attempts the impression with a muffled, swollen mouth, and Ilana notes 'the gauze in your mouth adds authenticity.'
jokes.explanation: Primarily a visual gag that would play better on screen — the muffled impression has inherent physical comedy. 'The gauze adds authenticity' is a smart written line supporting the visual.

jokes.text: Abbi's Drew Barrymore impression: 'I love Jimmy Fallon. Um, I have my own makeup line... My production company, Flower Films, was founded in 1999 with the popular film, Whip It.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is doing a Drew Barrymore impression with gauze in her mouth.
jokes.punchline: Flower Films was founded in 1995, not 1999 — and Ilana cuts in to correct this ('It was 1995!') with more Drew Barrymore expertise than Abbi, undermining Abbi's own impression.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Ilana knowing more Drew Barrymore trivia than Abbi mid-impression, which is funnier than the impression itself. 'She would love this impression' from Ilana is also a lovely piece of delusional friendship support. The date correction is the comedic peak.

jokes.text: Ilana, discussing Jeremy's thumbs-up text: 'He jerks off to you all the time, I sense those things. I know sexual tension when I feel it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is analyzing a simple thumbs-up emoji from Jeremy.
jokes.punchline: Ilana escalates from 'he likes you' to a confident assertion that she senses his masturbatory habits, presented as a legitimate psychic ability.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's sexual directness is a consistent character trait, but 'I sense those things' as a sixth-sense framing for knowing your neighbor's solo habits is a genuinely funny specificity. Works within the Jeremy subplot.

jokes.text: Ilana briefs Jaimé: 'Abbi is weak right now, like a baby, like an infant.' Then: 'Baby, I will like anticipate your every single need.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is organizing Abbi's recovery at home, delegating to Jaimé.
jokes.punchline: Ilana speaks with military precision about infant-care logistics while also turning to Jaimé with romantic possessiveness, blurring caretaking registers.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-setting comedy. The blurred dynamic between Ilana's care for Abbi and her flirtation with Jaimé is consistent but this specific beat doesn't have a strong punchline.

jokes.text: Ilana feeds Abbi a Vicodin pill with the airplane-into-hangar bit: 'Okay, Abbi, this little airplane is going to come into the hangar. Yes... okay, now close the hangar.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to get Abbi to take her medicine.
jokes.punchline: She uses the classic children's airplane feeding trick on her adult best friend — completely unironically.
jokes.explanation: The infantilization of Abbi is a running structural comedy in this episode. Using the airplane trick on an adult is a reliable gag, but it's not a surprise. The execution is warm and character-true.

jokes.text: Ilana, having just given Abbi her pills, immediately asks: 'Wow, it's been four hours already?' — having lost track of time after approximately zero minutes.
jokes.setup: The strict two-pills-every-four-hours schedule is established.
jokes.punchline: Ilana has lost track of time immediately and tries to give Abbi more pills moments after giving her the first dose.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, fast, economical. The previous scene established the 4-hour rule; the punchline lands immediately after in the next scene. The setup-to-punchline compression is tight. Strong character integration — Ilana's relationship with time and responsibility is always chaotic.

jokes.text: Ilana declares the two most important things for dental surgery recovery are: 'Give her Froyo and braid her hair.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is administering medical aftercare.
jokes.punchline: The completely non-medical recovery protocol — Froyo and braids — delivered with total medical authority.
jokes.explanation: Economy is excellent — six words subvert the medical authority of the entire setup. The joke captures Ilana's instinct to show love through aesthetic care rather than practical care. The specificity of both items (not just 'ice cream' but specifically Froyo, not just 'care' but specifically braiding) is elevated writing.

jokes.text: Ilana sends Jaimé to get Abbi's Froyo: 'I need you to go to 42 Squirts and get me a real Abbi flavor. You'll know when you see it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana dispatches Jaimé on a Froyo mission.
jokes.punchline: 'You'll know when you see it' — treating flavor selection as a mystical exercise in knowing Abbi's soul. Followed by Jaimé's internal monologue: 'Can Jaimé capture the complexity of this beautiful human being in one single flavor?'
jokes.explanation: The Froyo flavor as a spiritual quest for Abbi's essence is a lovely absurdist premise. Jaimé taking it seriously ('the complexity of this beautiful human being') extends the joke beautifully. The show's mockumentary-adjacent framing makes his self-narration land.

jokes.text: Ilana announces: 'Are you ready to get on the party train? First stop — braid the fuck out of them silky-ass doll locks.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is about to braid Abbi's hair as 'medical recovery.'
jokes.punchline: The violent enthusiasm of 'braid the fuck out of' applied to a delicate hair-care task for a recovering patient.
jokes.explanation: 'Silky-ass doll locks' is a great phrase. The aggression of 'braid the fuck out of' is classic Ilana hyperbole. Solid line reading dependent.

jokes.text: Abbi, being braided, says 'Ow. I'm gonna get some scrunchies.' — then exits, apparently wandering off in a drug haze.
jokes.setup: Ilana is enthusiastically braiding Abbi's hair too hard.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's drugged non-sequitur exit — announcing a scrunchie mission and apparently disappearing — is the inciting incident for her whole solo Whole Foods odyssey.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-comedy. The visual of a high, bandaged Abbi walking out mid-braid to get scrunchies is funny on screen. Not a joke with strong craft architecture but a good physical comedy beat.

jokes.text: Ilana plays 'Would You Rather' with drugged Abbi: 'Would you rather lick a dead man's penis, or have sex with the same person for the rest of your life?' Abbi: 'Penis.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is entertaining Abbi during recovery.
jokes.punchline: The 'Would You Rather' options are asymmetrically disgusting — a dead man's penis vs. monogamy — and Abbi immediately, unhesitatingly chooses the corpse option. The framing treats monogamy as the obviously worse fate.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely excellent joke construction. The asymmetry is the entire joke — a dead man's penis is objectively horrifying, but the show frames monogamy as the more frightening option for both Ilana (who asked) and Abbi (who answers instantly). Abbi's one-word 'Penis' is the perfect deadpan delivery. Deeply character-integrated — Ilana's fear of commitment and Abbi's people-pleasing nature (she just goes with whatever Ilana implies). Quotable, shocking, and shows these characters' values in a single exchange. Meets the 8.0 threshold.

jokes.text: 'Would you rather eat ice cream for five hours straight, or shoot somebody in the knee?' Abbi: 'Knee.'
jokes.setup: Continuing the Would You Rather game.
jokes.punchline: Again the mundane option (eating ice cream) loses to random violence (shooting someone in the knee). Abbi's instant answer is funnier because she clearly has no filter left.
jokes.explanation: Slightly weaker than the first WYR because the asymmetry is less extreme — ice cream is pleasant, not horrifying. But the pattern is established and Abbi's instant 'Knee' still lands. Good escalation of the bit.

jokes.text: Ilana describes firecrackers: 'It was what we were on that time that dude jerked off on your pocketbook in Bryant Park. He was cool.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining what firecrackers (weed edibles) are to Abbi.
jokes.punchline: The shared reference is a traumatic/bizarre event (a stranger ejaculated on Abbi's purse in a park) which Ilana offhandedly calls 'cool.' Her casualness about this is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The offhand reference to a shared nightmarish memory as a fond nostalgic marker ('he was cool') is exceptional character work. It tells us everything about their friendship — they have a whole catalogue of insane experiences, and Ilana's rating system is deeply broken. 'He was cool' as the closing evaluation of a public masturbator is one of the most Ilana lines in the episode. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana is telling Abbi not to message Jeremy — then we cut to Ilana apparently encouraging Abbi to leave Jeremy a love voicemail.
jokes.setup: Ilana told Abbi earlier to be open about her feelings for Jeremy.
jokes.punchline: Ilana panics when she realizes Abbi actually did it — 'Yeah, but not while you're jacked up on Uncle Sam pills' — revealing Ilana's advice was always conditional on sobriety.
jokes.explanation: 'Uncle Sam pills' is a characteristically Ilana way of saying government-prescribed medicine. The structural irony of Ilana's advice backfiring works but isn't a major comedic moment.

jokes.text: Abbi, fully drugged, picks up her own phone and starts leaving Jeremy a voicemail declaring her feelings: 'You know what, Jeremy? I will say it again. I like you. I want us to go out and...'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been hyping up Abbi's crush on Jeremy. Abbi, on Vicodin and weed firecrackers, picks up the phone.
jokes.punchline: She actually does it — calls Jeremy and declares her feelings in a voicemail — the thing she'd never do sober.
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe setup. The situational irony is solid: the very thing Abbi would never do while coherent, she does while incoherent. The pause before 'I will say it again' implies she has already called once, escalating the horror.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Yeah, but not while you're jacked-up on Uncle Sam pills.' Then: 'And don't you worry about leaving that message for Jeremy. You are gonna forget that in seven minutes.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is upset that Ilana told her to go for it with Jeremy.
jokes.punchline: 'Uncle Sam pills' is a callback to Lincoln's pharma rant. 'You're gonna forget in seven minutes' — a false reassurance that doesn't help Abbi and is probably untrue.
jokes.explanation: 'Uncle Sam pills' is a good callback to Lincoln's speech. 'Seven minutes' is a funny precise number for how long Abbi's memory will survive. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Jaimé's internal monologue while selecting Froyo toppings: extended deliberation over Cap'n Crunch ('too chemically'), dark chocolate chip ('too crunchy for Abbi right now'), gummy candies ('these do not go with the Froyo'), and GORP ('Good ol' raisins and peanuts — why would we want all raisins and peanuts?').
jokes.setup: Jaimé has been sent to find the flavor that represents Abbi's essence.
jokes.punchline: Jaimé's catastrophically serious approach to a minor grocery task — narrated with pseudo-sommelier intensity — escalates as he rejects every option.
jokes.explanation: The bit runs slightly long but the individual rejections are funny — especially 'too crunchy for Abbi right now' showing real dental-surgery consideration. The GORP definition is charming. Economy is the weak point.

jokes.text: Jaimé, alone in the Froyo shop, spontaneously begins singing an original song: '♪ Abbi dabbi dab my beautiful queen she's my beautiful queen she's... ♪'
jokes.setup: Jaimé is supposed to be selecting Froyo.
jokes.punchline: He composes and performs an original Abbi-themed ballad in the middle of a yogurt shop, completely absorbed in his devotion.
jokes.explanation: The spontaneous tribute song in a frozen yogurt shop is a great absurdist character beat for Jaimé. His devotion to Abbi is both sweet and unhinged, which is the show's consistent tone. A visual/performance gag that works.

jokes.text: Ilana has put Abbi in a meditation pose with candles as 'How To With Dr. Whiz' while Abbi is post-surgery and Lincoln's reaction is: 'I would think that you could surmise from my initial reaction that she doesn't [look gorgeous].'
jokes.setup: Ilana is proud of how she's caring for Abbi.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's arrival and horrified reaction confirms what the audience can see: Abbi looks terrible, not 'gorgeous.' His overly formal sentence construction while genuinely alarmed is a great straight-man beat.
jokes.explanation: 'I would think you could surmise from my initial reaction' is a beautifully calibrated Lincoln line — overly precise diction while alarmed. The visual of Ilana having put a swollen post-surgery Abbi in a meditation setup would play very well on screen.

jokes.text: I would think that you could surmise from my initial reaction that she doesn't.
jokes.setup: Ilana asks 'Doesn't she look gorgeous?' about the Dr. Whiz setup
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's icily formal response — 'I would think that you could surmise from my initial reaction that she doesn't' — rather than a simple 'no'
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's measured over-articulation as a response to being driven absolutely insane is a strong character beat. The joke is in the formality of his phrasing — he's suppressing a scream through vocabulary. Very good Lincoln moment.

jokes.text: Ilana tells Lincoln he's being loud when he's the concerned, normal one: 'Okay, jeez, you're the one who's being loud.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln tells Abbi she needs rest.
jokes.punchline: Ilana deflects his reasonable concern by accusing him of the behavior she was committing.
jokes.explanation: A competent but familiar irony beat. Ilana's deflection is predictable here — we know she never takes responsibility. Functional but not elevated.

jokes.text: Ilana propositions Lincoln: 'You wanna show me your old LL Cool J, your Lincoln Log?' Lincoln refuses. Ilana offers: 'I'll show you my tah-tahs.' Lincoln: 'Yes. Yes! Show me your penis.'
jokes.setup: Ilana declares she deserves a break from caregiving.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from Lincoln Log → tah-tahs → Lincoln asking to see her 'penis' (which she presumably doesn't have, making it a request for mutual exposure) — Lincoln's sudden enthusiasm followed by the anatomically confused final ask.
jokes.explanation: 'Lincoln Log' is a great nickname joke (Lincoln's name + the children's toy = his anatomy). His reversal from 'No!' to 'Yes! Yes!' in two seconds is a great character beat. 'Show me your penis' as a sign-off is absurd but earns its shock.

jokes.text: No! / I'll show you my tah-tahs. / Yes. Yes! / Show me your penis.
jokes.setup: Ilana propositions Lincoln in front of a semi-conscious post-surgery Abbi
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire negotiation over what body parts to show each other, escalating while Abbi is literally right there
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'Lincoln Log' to actual penis negotiation with Abbi present is good cringe comedy. Lincoln's initial 'No!' that quickly becomes 'Yes. Yes!' is a strong beat — his professional restraint collapses almost instantly.

jokes.text: Abbi wakes up to find Ilana and Lincoln apparently in flagrante: 'Oh, goodie, you're awake! We're gonna have so much fun! Let's go play outside!'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln were about to have sex (or already started) while 'caring for' post-surgery Abbi.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response to Abbi waking up is not embarrassment but immediate manic energy redirection: 'Let's go play outside!' — treating adult sex like a babysitting situation that just had an interruption.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from post-sex to 'LET'S GO PLAY OUTSIDE' is a great Ilana beat — same energy, different channel. The implication that having sex while babysitting an injured friend is fine but that we need to quickly redirect Abbi's attention like a child is perfectly inverted.

jokes.text: Lincoln observes Ilana's body art: 'Man, I hate to see it, but you got boyfriend penis, bro.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has drawn a face on her torso using her own body parts.
jokes.punchline: 'Boyfriend penis' — a term implying Lincoln's genitalia is relationship-coded and losing its edge — delivered with resigned sadness ('I hate to see it').
jokes.explanation: 'Boyfriend penis' is a highly original coinage — a concept that didn't exist before this scene and that many viewers will immediately recognize. The 'I hate to see it' adds genuine pathos. Lincoln's resigned self-awareness about his domestication is a great character note. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Ilana has drawn a torso-face and Abbi, Lincoln, and Ilana try to figure out who it is. Extended bit where nobody knows, culminating in: 'Maybe Abbi'll know.'
jokes.setup: Ilana drew an old-person face on her torso and is proud of it.
jokes.punchline: The excruciating beat where nobody — including the artist — can identify who it is. Ilana's own creation is unrecognizable to her. The resolution of 'Maybe Abbi'll know' treats a sleeping, recovering person as the art critic.
jokes.explanation: The bit runs slightly long. The awkward silence between the three characters trying to identify the drawing is the comedic engine. Lincoln's 'I'm guessing an old person cause the eyes are low and swinging... but in a way that makes me hard' is the standout line within this bit.

jokes.text: Lincoln on the torso drawing: 'I'm guessing an old person 'cause the eyes are low and swinging and drooping, but in a way that makes me hard. I never thought that an old people face would make me hard.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln is trying to identify Ilana's torso art.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's genuine sexual self-discovery in real-time — discovering he's aroused by elderly facial features — delivered with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: the absurdity of the premise (drooping eyes = low-hanging torso features), Lincoln's genuine innocent discovery, and the complete sincerity of 'I never thought that an old people face would make me hard.' This is the kind of line that gets quoted. The character commitment to a ridiculous position is the hallmark of good comedy. High originality — this specific configuration of ideas is unlike anything else.

jokes.text: Ilana reassures Lincoln about his new arousal: 'You are gonna be an old dude with so many boners in a non-creepy way. That's hot.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln has discovered the torso-face makes him hard.
jokes.punchline: 'In a non-creepy way' is doing enormous work — acknowledging that being aroused by old people faces could be creepy while insisting it isn't, with zero justification.
jokes.explanation: The qualifier 'in a non-creepy way' is the entire joke — and it's a good one. Ilana's instinct to reassure and normalize is deployed here in a situation that cannot be normalized. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ilana confesses to Lincoln she's been lying about the Vicodin schedule: 'I lied, I fucked lied, dude! I gave her the strongest form of weed possible. Other than dabbing.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has gone missing; Lincoln tries to reassure Ilana she'll be okay on her meds schedule.
jokes.punchline: Ilana has not been administering Vicodin — she replaced it with weed. 'Other than dabbing' is a surreal qualifier that suggests there exists a hierarchy of weed potency she considered and ranked.
jokes.explanation: 'Other than dabbing' is the perfect Ilana qualification — she shows her work on the weed hierarchy with a single phrase, implying she made an informed decision to use the second-strongest form. The revelation itself is a great plot bomb. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I haven't smoked once, I didn't even vape. This is all my fault.' Then: 'I lied, I fucked lied, dude!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is enumerating her sacrifices during the caretaking day.
jokes.punchline: The pathos of 'I haven't smoked once' as evidence of dedication (immediately followed by the confession that she drugged Abbi with firecrackers) is the irony. Then 'I lied, I fucked lied, dude!' as self-incrimination.
jokes.explanation: The self-contradicting sequence (I'm so responsible / I fucked up completely) is solid character comedy. Ilana's simultaneous pride in not vaping and guilt over the firecrackers creates a nice ironic mess.

jokes.text: Wandering, high Abbi finds Whole Foods and calls it 'the most magical place in all the land.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is heavily stoned and wandering Brooklyn post-surgery.
jokes.punchline: She treats a gentrified grocery store with the wonder of a fairytale kingdom. 'The most magical place in all the land' applied to Whole Foods.
jokes.explanation: Solid situational comedy — the Whole Foods-as-wonderland is a reliable bit for a stoned young urbanite. 'The neighborhood's really changed' from her guide is a nice NYC-gentrification button.

jokes.text: Ilana, searching for Abbi in a store, describes her: 'Did you see a beautiful, sumptuous model-type come through? Long silky hair like a horse's main?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is frantically searching for missing Abbi.
jokes.punchline: The description is wildly over-romanticized — 'sumptuous model-type' and 'horse's main [mane]' — while Abbi is actually a post-surgery patient with gauze in her mouth and swollen cheeks.
jokes.explanation: The gap between Ilana's description of Abbi and Abbi's actual condition is the joke, and it's fueled by how deeply Ilana sees Abbi as beautiful. 'Horse's main' (mane misspoken) adds a characteristically imprecise Ilana touch.

jokes.text: Store employee: 'The only thing I see is that crazy woman who bought two Yoo-hoos and dropped a bottle on the floor.' Ilana: 'Yoo-hoo.' Employee: 'Yeah, that's what I just said.' Ilana: 'Thank you so much. Get out of my store, get out of my—'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks about a woman matching Abbi's description.
jokes.punchline: Ilana interprets 'Yoo-hoo' (the drink brand) as the greeting 'yoo-hoo' and uses it as an acknowledgment. The employee corrects her; she thanks him and immediately tells HIM to get out of HIS store.
jokes.explanation: This is a tightly constructed wordplay joke with a second layer: the misunderstanding of 'Yoo-hoo' is funny, but Ilana then IMMEDIATELY evicts the employee from his own store after receiving helpful information. The structure is three-beat perfect: confusion → correction → Ilana's inexplicable aggression toward a helpful stranger. 'Get out of my store' when it isn't her store is a wonderfully specific piece of Ilana chaos. Meets the 8.0 threshold clearly.

jokes.text: Ilana chases the retreating employee: 'Punk. Dick, bitch.'
jokes.setup: She's just told a helpful stranger to get out of his own store.
jokes.punchline: She then escalates with insults at the person who helped her, using a three-part ascending curse that's almost rhythmically satisfying.
jokes.explanation: Solid button to the previous joke. The escalation from eviction to insults is perfectly Ilana, and the three-curse structure has a rhythm. But it only works as a capper, not as a standalone joke.

jokes.text: Abbi, high and loose in Whole Foods, narrates her shopping: 'Hearts of palm! Abbi, buy me that. I love hearts of palm... Bulk granola! Buy me that... Manuka honey, so reasonably priced! Buy me that, Abbi!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is hallucinating/dissociating and narrating from outside herself.
jokes.punchline: She is talking to herself in third person, commanding herself to buy upscale health food items with the urgency of a child at a candy store, while also recognizing the honey is 'so reasonably priced' (it isn't).
jokes.explanation: 'Manuka honey, so reasonably priced!' is the best individual line — the irony of a stoned person on surgery drugs recognizing Whole Foods pricing as reasonable is a sharp social observation. The third-person self-addressing is a great physical/performance bit. Good sustained comedic energy.

jokes.text: Abbi instructs herself: 'Ooh, earth-friendly cereals. Knock 'em over, Abbi. They wanna be on the ground.'
jokes.setup: Abbi continues her self-narrated Whole Foods rampage.
jokes.punchline: 'They wanna be on the ground' — Abbi's impaired logic convincing herself the boxes of cereal have an ambition to fall. The anthropomorphization of cereal boxes is peak drug-logic.
jokes.explanation: 'They wanna be on the ground' is a lovely, economical piece of drug-logic writing. It perfectly captures the moral reasoning of an impaired person seeking permission for destruction. Excellent economy — five words justify property damage with complete internal logic.

jokes.text: Abbi announces: 'Next up, probiotics, a Neti Pot and a bag of douche.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is narrating her Whole Foods shopping list.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from mundane health items to 'a bag of douche' — the product name landing with comedic bluntness.
jokes.explanation: The list joke with 'bag of douche' as the cap is a familiar structure. It's executed competently but the escalation isn't as surprising as it could be.

jokes.text: Abbi checks out $1,487.56 worth of Whole Foods items.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been running through Whole Foods buying everything.
jokes.punchline: The total is revealed: nearly fifteen hundred dollars on Whole Foods in a drug haze. The specific amount ($1,487.56) is funnier than a round number.
jokes.explanation: The reveal lands because the specific cents ($1,487.56) suggest an itemized reality — she really bought all those things. A round number would be lazier. Good escalation payoff.

jokes.text: Ilana (or Abbi's friend via phone) encourages Abbi to use her credit card: 'You got it because you thought you needed more than one card to be an adult, right? Well, now's your chance to be one!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is about to pay $1,487 while high.
jokes.punchline: The 'this is your moment to be an adult' speech is deployed at maximum irony — spending $1,500 on impulse Whole Foods items while post-surgery-high is the opposite of adult behavior.
jokes.explanation: The irony of 'adulthood' being invoked for this purchase is good but not brilliantly constructed. The Starwood points detail adds a nice realistic texture of young adult financial anxiety.

jokes.text: After the purchase: 'Quick, let's get this into a bunker before the big one hits.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just spent $1,487 at Whole Foods.
jokes.punchline: She frames getting the groceries home as prepper survivalism — 'before the big one hits' — treating health food as emergency preparedness supplies.
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist tag to the purchase. 'Before the big one hits' is a funny paranoid framing for Whole Foods prep. Effective but brief.

jokes.text: Ilana finds Abbi via a credit card fraud call: the automated voice confirms the purchase at 'Whole Foods in Gowanus, Brooklyn' and closes with: 'From your clear answer, I can tell you are Abbi Abrams, and this was not a fraudulent charge.'
jokes.setup: Ilana gets a fraud alert on Abbi's account.
jokes.punchline: The automated system interprets Ilana's 'Oh my God, of course, she's hungry as fuck' as a clear, confident identity verification. The script's internal logic of the fraud line treating panicked expletive as affirmation is sharp.
jokes.explanation: The automated system treating swearing profusely as clear confirmation is a great absurdist joke grounded in recognizable phone automation comedy. 'From your clear answer' is perfectly bureaucratic language for the situation.

jokes.text: The credit card hold music: '♪ On the flip side you crazy bitch ♪'
jokes.setup: The fraud prevention call has just ended.
jokes.punchline: The hold music is apparently a song with the lyric 'on the flip side you crazy bitch' — an absurd choice of corporate hold music that functions as editorial commentary.
jokes.explanation: The timing is perfect — the call ends and the hold music editorial is perfectly timed commentary on the chaos. Economy is very high, the joke is entirely in the lyric choice. A strong absurdist gag.

jokes.text: Ilana, returning to the street, says: 'Damn, this neighborhood is changing.'
jokes.setup: Ilana exits the store she just got thrown out of.
jokes.punchline: Gentrification joke — 'the neighborhood is changing' as a callback to Abbi saying the same about Gowanus Whole Foods — but delivered as if Ilana hasn't just been evicted from a Whole Foods herself.
jokes.explanation: The gentrification callback is a thin joke used as a scene transition button. Economy is good but the comedic concept is underdeveloped.

jokes.text: Abbi spots Ilana outside Whole Foods and screams 'Oh fuck, it's Strega Nona the weed witch!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is stoned and wandering. Ilana arrives to find her.
jokes.punchline: Abbi — so high she doesn't recognize her best friend — identifies Ilana as a character from a children's picture book. 'Strega Nona the weed witch' adds the drug modifier to the children's story character.
jokes.explanation: A great callback that works independently — Ilana named her weed preparation 'Strega Nona,' and Abbi, completely fried, hallucinates Ilana as the titular children's book witch. The modifier 'weed witch' is both accurate to Ilana and perfectly specific. The callback elevates a solid standalone joke into something with narrative resonance. 'Strega Nona the weed witch' is among the most quotable lines of the episode.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Peace, mortals.' As she approaches Abbi.
jokes.setup: Ilana has found Abbi, who is terrified of her.
jokes.punchline: Instead of reassuring Abbi she's her friend, Ilana leans into the witch narrative: 'Peace, mortals.'
jokes.explanation: Two words. 'Peace, mortals' — Ilana playing along with the witch misidentification rather than correcting Abbi — is perfectly character-integrated (Ilana would absolutely lean into this), deeply economical, and genuinely funny. It's also the single funniest possible thing she could say. Exceptional economy score.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'God dammit, my manuka honey!' — noticing she lost her $1,487 of groceries.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been found outside Whole Foods, apparently without her groceries.
jokes.punchline: Of everything she bought, the thing Abbi mourns is the manuka honey — a callback to her declaring it 'so reasonably priced.'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of manuka honey as the mourned item is the callback payoff — it's the most Whole Foods item. 'Goddammit, my manuka honey' has the rhythm of real grief and the content of absurdity.

jokes.text: Abbi throws Bingo Bronson the stuffed animal at 'Strega Nona' as a defensive weapon. Ilana: 'I got you, Bingo Bronson!' Abbi: 'I love you, Bingo Bronson!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is terrified of Ilana/Strega Nona.
jokes.punchline: She uses her comfort stuffed animal as a weapon, both characters immediately declare love for it, and the toss is described as 'airball' — it doesn't even land.
jokes.explanation: Bingo Bronson's journey from dental comfort object to street weapon is a nice structural callback. 'Airball' as the attack descriptor is a quietly excellent detail. Both characters loving the stuffed animal equally is warm and funny.

jokes.text: Lincoln, after the crisis: 'I feel like an old-timey doctor right now making house calls. I should get an apothecary bag, right? Oh, my God, I am so horny right now, it hurts.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln has just done medical triage on Abbi post-adventure.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln pivots mid-sentence from professional medical satisfaction to confessing acute arousal, treating horniness as a medical symptom equivalent to his doctoring.
jokes.explanation: The tonal pivot from 'apothecary bag' professional satisfaction to 'so horny it hurts' is a great character beat for Lincoln — his sincerity about both is equally absolute. 'It hurts' as the qualifier for horniness is unexpectedly effective. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana, responding to Lincoln's horniness revelation: 'Oh, yeah? It hurts.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln says he's very horny.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's simple, approving confirmation — 'It hurts' — as if she's receiving a medical report and validating his diagnosis.
jokes.explanation: Two-word response that affirms Lincoln with the gravity of a clinical confirmation. Maximum economy. The joke is the total absence of irony in Ilana's validation.

jokes.text: Jaimé is still at the Froyo shop at what appears to be closing time, being told to pick any flavor: 'Just pick any flavor, they're all chemicals.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé was sent for Froyo hours ago.
jokes.punchline: He is still there — the entire episode's action has occurred while Jaimé has been unable to commit to a Froyo flavor. The employee's defeated 'they're all chemicals' is the perfect exasperated payoff.
jokes.explanation: This is the best structural joke in the episode. The payoff works because it's been building since minute 8 — the entire show has happened while Jaimé paralyzed himself with flavor analysis. 'They're all chemicals' is the employee's total philosophical surrender after Jaimé's spiritual quest. The time-collapse (Jaimé's obsessive commitment vs. the speed of Abbi's odyssey) is excellent structural comedy. Meets and slightly exceeds the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Jaimé finally chooses: 'It's the Gummi Bears.'
jokes.setup: Jaimé has spent the entire episode agonizing over the flavor that captures Abbi's essence.
jokes.punchline: After all the spiritual deliberation, the answer is: Gummi Bears. It's both deeply anticlimactic and strangely perfect.
jokes.explanation: Four words pay off thirty-plus minutes of buildup. 'It's the Gummi Bears' is tonally perfect — said with the gravity of a great revelation, about Gummi Bears. The economy is nearly perfect (a 9.5 is warranted — you could not do this in fewer words). It's funny because it's both completely wrong as an 'essence of Abbi' answer and also somehow not wrong at all. Excellent structural comedy.

jokes.text: Jeremy confronts Abbi about her voicemail: 'That really long voice mail you left me where you asked me out.'
jokes.setup: Abbi left a love declaration voicemail while heavily drugged.
jokes.punchline: She has to confront it while still somewhat impaired, trying to walk it back.
jokes.explanation: The cringe payoff of the episode's romantic subplot. Works as structure but the individual lines aren't particularly sharp — it's more emotional setup for the resolution than strong joke construction.

jokes.text: Abbi tries to explain away the voicemail and then mimes answering a fake phone call: 'Hello? Hi. No, why would I throw up on my clothes? I'm not crazy... yeah, I am Jewish... it has nothing to do with it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to avoid the Jeremy conversation.
jokes.punchline: She resurrects the fake phone call escape tactic from the opening scene — but this version is even more unhinged, the imaginary conversation taking unexpected turns (Judaism, vomiting on clothes).
jokes.explanation: The callback to the fake phone call is excellently deployed — same avoidance tactic, completely different (and more surreal) content. 'I am Jewish... it has nothing to do with it' is a great non-sequitur that suggests Abbi's invented caller is accusing her of something she finds offensive. The escalation from the first fake call to this one shows character growth in the wrong direction.

jokes.text: Jeremy, Abbi, and Ilana all yell 'Listen!' over each other trying to get a word in, then Jeremy breaks through: 'I would love to go out with you on an official... thing.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are both spiraling in front of Jeremy.
jokes.punchline: Jeremy cuts through the chaos with a declaration that trails off into '... thing' — unable to commit to the word 'date' even in a romantic moment.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'thing' instead of 'date' is well-calibrated character comedy for Jeremy — he's enthusiastic but equally awkward. The chaos leading into the earnest declaration gives it a good emotional release. 'Official... thing' is a nice specific bit of millennial romantic language aversion.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'And I loved your Drew Barrymore impersonation. Amazing.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy heard the voicemail Abbi left while heavily drugged.
jokes.punchline: The voicemail apparently included Abbi's Drew Barrymore impression — done with gauze in her mouth while high — and Jeremy found it charming enough to mention specifically.
jokes.explanation: The callback works beautifully — the muffled, gauze-impeded impression from earlier is retroactively revealed to have been captured on voicemail and considered 'amazing.' Jeremy's sincere compliment suggests he either has terrible taste or finds Abbi's chaos endearing. The payoff is warm and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: After Jeremy leaves, Abbi processes: 'So, I guess I'll give you a call. Yeah, that was... yeah. Okay. Okay, ring her up... me.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy has just asked Abbi out and left.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's internal processing is narrated aloud, including accidentally addressing herself in third person ('ring her up... me') — a last residue of the drugged dissociation from the Whole Foods scene.
jokes.explanation: 'Ring her up... me' is an excellent closing joke — it's character-true (Abbi is overwhelmed), structurally tight (the third-person self-address callbacks the drug sequence), and emotionally resonant. The 'yeah, that was... yeah' captures authentic post-romantic-moment word failure. Strong closing beat.
